coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dot tailored calcium alginate hydrogel for pH responsive controlled delivery of vancomycin
coredata.dc:creator: Sarkar, Niladri
Sahoo, Gyanaranjan
Das, Rashmita
Prusty, Gyanaranjan
Swain, Sarat K.
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we demonstrate the preparation of highly luminescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from Aloe vera leaf gel; in just 2h at 250°C through carbonization pathway. The prepared CQDs are structurally characterized with high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), hydrodynamic diameter, surface polarity, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Raman, UV–visible absorption spectrophotometry and fluorescence spectroscopy. The functional carbon nanoparticles are observed as non-cytotoxic materials. The biocompatibility, less cytotoxicity and high aqueous dispersibility of as-synthesized CQDs are motivated to design carbon quantum dot (CQD) tailored calcium alginate (CA) hydrogel films with an aim to controlled delivery of glycopeptides antibiotic vancomycin in the gastrointestinal tract (GI). With CQD, the drug loading capacity of CA/CQD film is increased to 89% from 38% (CA film), whereas; with β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) the vancomycin uptake capacity is increased more, 96%. The release of vancomycin through CA/CQD film is more pronounced at pH1.5, close to the pH of the stomach and it is found that in pH1.5 with β-CD, the release rate of vancomycin is lowered, 56% in 120h. The high drug uptake capacity (96%) and lower release rate (56% in 120h) of CA/CQD hydrogel film in pH1.5 with β-CD can be used for its applicability as drug delivery vehicle for controlled release of vancomycin into the stomach region and therefore it can offer a potential option for oral administration of vancomycin.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence
Carbon quantum dots (CQDs)
Ca-alginate
Drug delivery

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of Carbon Quantum Dot Tailored Calcium algInate (CA-CQD) Nanohybrid Films
Preparation of Vancomycin and β-CD Associated Vancomycin Loaded Carbon Quantum Dot Tailored Calcium Alginate (CA-CQD/VA & CA-CQD/β-VA) Nanohybrid Films
In-vitro Drug Release Measurement Through CA and CA-CQD Nanohybrid Films
Optical Properties of Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs)
In-vitro Cytotoxicity Assessment and Optical Imaging of Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) in HeLa Cells
Micro-structural, Optical and Swelling Behaviors of Carbon Dot Tailored Ca-alginate (CA-CQD) Film
Thermal Behavior of Calcium Alginate Film and CQD Coated Alginate Film
Vancomycin (VA) Drug Loading and pH Responsive Delivery Profiles of CA and CA-CQD Nanohybrid Film
In vitro Release of VA Through (1:1) Host-Guest Complex of β-CD and VA From CA and CA-CQD Nanohybrid Films at pH1.5

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis procedure of CQDs from Aloe vera leaf gel.
Loading scheme of vancomycin (VA) and β-cyclodextrin associated vancomycin (β-VA) within CA-CQD film network [(a), (c)]; The possible chemical interactions in CA-CQD/VA and CA-CQD/β-VA[(b) and (d)].

TEM micrographs[(a), (b) and (c)]of CQDs in different scale of magnification; HRTEM micrograph (d) of single CQD; lattice fringe image (e) of CQD; SAED image (f) of CQD. [Inset of (a): particle size distribution profiles of as-synthesized CQDs].
XPS spectrum (a) of CQDs, the deconvoluted XPS spectrum of C (1s), O (1s) and N (1s) of CQDs [(b), (c) and (d)], FTIR spectrum (e) of synthesized CQDs, Raman spectrum (f) of synthesized CQDs.

UV–visible absorption spectrum (a) of CQDs [inset: digital image of CQD solution in day light and in presence of UV lamp (λex =360nm)]; the photoluminescence and excitation spectrum (a) of as synthesized CQDs; excitation dependent (λex) photoluminescence spectra (b) of CQDs; fluorescence decay curve (c) of aqueous dispersion of CQDs; upconverted photoluminescence (d) of CQDs.
(a) PL emission of synthesized CQDs in different pH medium, [(b) and (c)] digital images of pH varied CQD solution in day light and under UV lamp (λex =360nm) respectively.
(a) Bright field image, (b) fluorescence microscopy image, (c) merged image, (d) cell viability (%) of synthesized CQDs in HeLa cells.

Digital images of CA-CQD nanohybrid film (a) in day light and (b) under UV lamp; swelling properties (c) of CA and CA-CQD film at pH7; scanning electron micrographs (SEM) (d) of CA-CQD film; HRTEM image (e) of CA-CQD nanohybrid film (inset: SAED pattern of CA-CQD film); swelling variation (f) of CA and CA-CQD film under different pH condition.
TG curves of Ca-alginate film and CQD coated Ca-alginate nanohybrid film [inset: (a) digital images of CA film and (b) CA-CQD film].
pH responsive drug delivery of vancomycin (VA) from CA film (a) and CA-CQD nanohybrid film (b) for a period of 120h.

(a) Cumulative release (%) of vancomycin from CA and CA-CQD nanohybrid film, (b) Cumulative release (%) of β-cyclodextrin associated vancomycin (β-VA) from CA and CA-CQD nanohybrid film, (c) effect of β-CD on the % cumulative release of vancomycin through CA and CA-CQD film network. (in sets of (a), (b), (c) and (d) are digital images of the respected drug release mediums).

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dot tailored calcium alginate hydrogel for pH responsive controlled delivery of vancomycin

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Veer Surendra Sai University of Technology, Burla, Sambalpur 768018, Odisha, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Veer Surendra Sai University of Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we demonstrate the preparation of highly luminescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from Aloe vera leaf gel; in just 2h at 250°C through carbonization pathway. The prepared CQDs are structurally characterized with high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), hydrodynamic diameter, surface polarity, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Raman, UV–visible absorption spectrophotometry and fluorescence spectroscopy. The functional carbon nanoparticles are observed as non-cytotoxic materials. The biocompatibility, less cytotoxicity and high aqueous dispersibility of as-synthesized CQDs are motivated to design carbon quantum dot (CQD) tailored calcium alginate (CA) hydrogel films with an aim to controlled delivery of glycopeptides antibiotic vancomycin in the gastrointestinal tract (GI). With CQD, the drug loading capacity of CA/CQD film is increased to 89% from 38% (CA film), whereas; with β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) the vancomycin uptake capacity is increased more, 96%. The release of vancomycin through CA/CQD film is more pronounced at pH1.5, close to the pH of the stomach and it is found that in pH1.5 with β-CD, the release rate of vancomycin is lowered, 56% in 120h. The high drug uptake capacity (96%) and lower release rate (56% in 120h) of CA/CQD hydrogel film in pH1.5 with β-CD can be used for its applicability as drug delivery vehicle for controlled release of vancomycin into the stomach region and therefore it can offer a potential option for oral administration of vancomycin.

ce:section.ce:para: Chemically cross-linked three dimensional (3-D) networks of polymeric hydrogels are highly recognized for their wide range of applications, including tissue engineering (Khademhosseini and Langer, 2007), cell culture (Bhattacharya et al., 2012), drug delivery (Bhattarai et al., 2010), water treatment (Gao et al., 2013) and soft machining (Nandu et al., 1993). In connection to the biomedical application, researchers are highly motivated to fabricate stimuli responsive hydrogels from natural resources (Prabaharan and Mano, 2006). Out of these, sodium alginate, a sodium salt of alginic acid is widely used for their hydrogel forming ability with bi-valent cations, such as Ca2+. Alginic acids are widely distributed in the cell walls of brown algae and capable of absorbing water of 200–300 times of its own weight. Basically, it is a copolymer of alternating blocks of 1,4 linked α-l-guluronic (G) and β-d-mannuronic (M) acid residues (Kuo and Ma, 2008). Ca-alginate based hydrogels are widely used in drug delivery purposes (Agarwal et al., 2015; Jana et al., 2016), as these are biocompatible, biodegradable and impart no immunogenic toxicity on administration (Becker et al., 2001).

In recent times, the intriguing combination of nanostructure materials with polymer has captured the focus of biomaterial research with the aiming to develop new types of drug delivery vehicles with enhanced delivery performances. The use of noble metal nanoparticles, such as silver and gold nanoparticles with alginate residues are reported for their enhanced performance in wound healing (Varaprasad et al., 2011) and drug delivery (Yadollahi et al., 2015; Kim and Randall Lee, 2006) applications. Although, the noble metal nanoparticles are not completely free from cytotoxicity (Prabhu and Poulose, 2012), on the other hand, a new nanostructure of fluorescent carbon, named carbon quantum dot (CQD) with extremely low size (<10nm) has stepped into the nano‑carbon family (Xu et al., 2004; Sun et al., 2006) and grabbed the lime-light in the emerging area of advanced research (Baker and Baker, 2010). The almost non-toxic, luminescent CQDs are also considered as the best alternative of toxic semiconductor QDs such as CdSe, CdS, Ag2S and PbSe in the field of biomedical application. Due to unique characteristics, such as tunable photoluminescence (Kang et al., 2009), photostability, low cytotoxicity, robust chemical inertness and excellent biocompatibility, these functional nanoparticles are widely used as promising candidate in bio-imaging (Liang et al., 2013; Hu et al., 2014; Hu et al., 2013), sensors (Nie et al., 2014; Sun et al., 2013; Qin et al., 2013a, 2013b), drug delivery (Wang et al., 2013; Lai et al., 2012), ink (Zhu et al., 2013a, 2013b; Wang et al., 2012), photocatalysis (Yu et al., 2014; Zhang et al., 2012b, 2012a; Ma et al., 2012) and optoelectronics (Tang et al., 2012; Li et al., 2011b, 2011a). The up-growing interest in CQD based technological application, compels the researchers to find out the simplest and ecofriendly strategy to fabricate luminescent CQDs. Among different synthetic methodologies, such as laser ablation (Li et al., 2010b, 2010c, 2010a), acidic oxidation (Liu et al., 2012b, 2012c, 2012a), pyrolysis (Wang et al., 2012; Lai et al., 2012), incomplete combustion (Liu et al., 2007), hydrothermal (Qu et al., 2013; Dong et al., 2013), plasma treatment (Gokus et al., 2009) and microwave/ultrasonic passivation (Liu et al., 2012b, 2012c, 2012a; Zhu et al., 2012a, 2012b) etc., carbonization technique is considered as the easiest and best way to provide CQDs with low cost. Not only the fabrication pathway, but also the choice of carbon source is an important factor for its technological applications.

Recently, natural resources are taking the charge over different synthetic precursors such as candle soot (Liu et al., 2007), paper ash (Wei et al., 2013), natural gas soot (Tian et al., 2009), graphene (Peng et al., 2012), tris (hydroxymethyl) aminomethane (Zhang et al., 2013), glycerols (Wang et al., 2011), poly ethylene glycol (PEG) (Fan et al., 2014) etc. Some natural sources are soy milk (Zhu et al., 2012a, 2012b), banana juice (De and Karak, 2013), coffee grounds (Jiang et al., 2014), saccharides (Sevilla and Fuertes, 2009), amino acids (Zhang et al., 2012b, 2012a), grass (Liu et al., 2012b, 2012c, 2012a), orange juice (Sahu et al., 2012), honey (Yang et al., 2014), Trapa bispinosa peel extract (Mewada et al., 2013), eggs (Wang et al., 2012), lauryl gallate (Hola et al., 2014), plant leaves (Zhu et al., 2013a, 2013b), bamboo leaves (Liu et al., 2014) etc. CQDs from natural sources are used in analytical sensing (Yu et al., 2015; Hu et al., 2013), preparing energy nanomaterials (Li et al., 2014) and other functionalized nanomaterials (Xue et al., 2015). Although, the use of different natural biomass as C-source has already been explored, but the use of aloe-vera for CQD preparation is very rare in the literature. Moreover, the fabrication of CQDs from aloe-vera leaf gel through carbonization pathway is still unexplored.

The mucilage gels of Aloe vera (Aloe barbendis Miller) are used since early times as anti-inflammatory, immune-modulator, antiseptic, wound healing, antiulcer, antitumor, antidiabetic and antioxidant materials (Tarameshloo et al., 2012). The high anti-oxidant activity of Aloe vera gel (Hu et al., 2003) over other synthetic anti-oxidants such as butylated hydroxyanisole (BHA), butylated hydroxytoluene (BHT) and tertiary butyl hydroquinone (TBHQ) makes it a popular choice in cosmetic world. Recently, Aloe vera plant is known to be used for HIV-AIDS treatment (Olatunya et al., 2012). It contains 98.5% water, uronic acid, 6 anti-septic agents, 23 polypeptides, 3 inflammatory agents, vitamin A and vitamin B12, different enzymes such as carboxypeptidase, bradykinase, catalase etc. Aloe vera gel also contains some phytochemicals like acetylated mannans, polymannans, anthraquinones C-glycosides and anthrone etc., along with lower % of nitrogen containing compounds. The highly luminescent functional carbon nanoparticles from natural sources are considered as safe option for cell imaging as they are almost non-toxic in nature with tunable photoluminescence phenomenon. At the same time, CQDs are coated onto the biopolymers to enhance their physiochemical properties, including mechanical, thermal, swelling and drug delivery responses (Konwar and Chowdhury, 2015; Gogoi and Chowdhury, 2014).

Vancomycin is the most important amphoteric, tricyclic glycopeptides antibiotic, used for treating serious infections, including osteomyelities, caused by Gram-positive bacteria such as Staphylococcus aureus (Lee et al., 2004). The antibacterial activity of vancomycin is due to their ability to form five point hydrogen bonds with terminal D-alanyl-D-alanine (D-Ala-D-Ala) moieties of 5′-N-acetylmuramyl-pentapeptide, a major component of bacterial cell wall (Gu et al., 2003). Moreover, this drug is considered as safe option to treat osteomyelities as it has no side effects to normal osteoblasts and skeletal cell as compared to other commonly used antimicrobial agents (Antoci et al., 2007). Instead of oral administration, vancomycin is injected intravenously to the patient. It is because of their poor absorption to gastrointestinal tract. However, only few investigations are reported to improve the gastrointestinal tract absorption of vancomycin by using liposomes (Uhl et al., 2017) and sodium glycolate as absorption promoters. On the other hand, recently 1:1 inclusion complex of vancomycin with β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) is reported for the controlled release of vancomycin (Zarif et al., 2012). The low cost β-CD is widely known for their ability to form inclusion complexes with lypophilic drug molecules through host-guest interaction. The “Tub shaped” β-CD entraps the lypophilic drug molecules within its hydrophobic cleft to improve the solubility, bio-availability and stability of the drug molecules (Del Valle, 2004). In case of amphoteric drug molecules, the hydrobic zones of the drug molecules are only entrapped into the cavity and form the inclusion complexes with β-CD. The controlled release of vancomycin into the gastrointestinal tract could be a better option to improve the efficacy of vancomycin instead of their poor adsorption to gastrointestinal tract.

In present investigation, ecofriendly carbonization and green technique is adopted to fabricate CQDs with high and tunable luminescent properties and low cytotoxicity from Aloe vera leaf gel. Structural characterization of the as-synthesized CQDs is carried out through TEM, FTIR, XRD, XPS and Raman spectroscopy. The influence of excitation wavelength, pH and solvent polarity on the photoluminescence property of the CQDs is studied in detail. Cytotoxicity of the prepared CQDs is investigated through MTT assay and its applicability as bio-imaging probes in three different cell lines such as HeLa cells, A-549 cells and HepG2 cells are also performed. As CQDs are functional nanomaterials, these are effectively embedded into the gelling network of Ca-alginate though simple solution casting technique to improve its physico-chemical properties. Structural characterization of the CA/CQD nanohybrid thin film is performed through TEM and SEM analysis. Interestingly, the fabricated CA/CQD nanohybrid thin film is examined for its applicability to deliver vancomycin with or without β-CD, in the gastrointestinal tract with the aim to eliminate the complicacy arising from the poor absorption of vancomycin in the GI tract and can be used for oral administration.

Aloe vera (Aloe barbadensis Mill.) leaves were collected from the potted plants of our University campus and washed with double distilled water for further use. Sodium alginate from brown marine algae was supplied by Sigma Aldrich, USA (viscosity≥10 cps, MW=100,000–350,000; G: M blocks=66:34). Calcium chloride was supplied by Rankem Laboratory Reagent, India. Vancomycin (VA) was obtained from Cheil Jedang, (Korea). Beta cyclodextrin (β-CD) was purchased from Sigma Aldrich, USA. Three different Cell lines (HeLa Cells, HepG2 Cells and A 549 Cells) were purchased from National Centre for Cell Science, Pune, India. Different solvents such as ethanol, acetone, DMF, n-hexane, DCM and chloroform (CHCl3) were purchased from Merck, India which were pure and used as such. Hydrochloric acid (HCl), acetic acid (AcOH) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) used for pH adjustment were of analytical grade and used as received. Dulbecco's modified Eagle medium (DMEM), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, purity 99.9%) and 3-(4, 5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2, 5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT, purity 98%) were supplied from Sigma Aldrich. All required solutions were prepared with double distilled water.

The preparation of highly luminescent CQD was typically described as follows. The fresh leaves of Aloe vera were first washed with double distilled water to remove the dust particles and other adherent impurities from the leaf surface. Then the epidermis or skin of the Aloe vera leaf was removed by a thin bladed knife. After that the leaf was sliced and washed again with double distilled water and dry in the open air. Then these pieces of Aloe vera leaf were placed in a ceramic crucible and carbonized at 250°C for 2h in a tube furnace at a heating rate of 10°C per minute. The dark black product was then allowed to cool at room temperature and then mechanically ground to form fine powders. Then 0.2g of the carbonized sample was added into 50mL of distilled water and made to disperse through ultrasonication for 30min at 120kHz/60W. The resultant black solution of CQD along with larger carbon particles was filtered with 0.22μm membrane, followed by centrifugation of the resultant filtrate at 15,000rpm for 30min to remove larger particles. The supernatant CQD solution was kept at 4°C for further characterization. The overall synthesis of water soluble CQDs is represented in Fig. 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Carbon Quantum Dot Tailored Calcium algInate (CA-CQD) Nanohybrid Films
ce:section.ce:para: Ca-alginate (CA) and CQD coated Ca-alginate (CA-CQD) nanohybrid films were prepared in a two step process by means of simple solution casting techniques. In the first step, Ca2+ ions were allowed to exchange with Na+ ions of Na-alginate in order to form a gelling network (egg box structure) of Ca-alginate. The size to charge ratio of divalent Ca2+ plays the main role to squeezing the mannuronate (M) and guluronate (G) residues of alginate through electrostatic interactions with the COO- groups of alginate (Russo et al., 2007). In our procedure, 20mL of 1% (w/v) freshly prepared sodium alginate solution was added quickly in 100mL of 0.1M CaCl2 solution under undisturbed conditions, resulting in instant formation of Ca2+ cross-linked alginate film. Then the as-prepared calcium alginate (CA) film was left in CaCl2 solution overnight to allow complete gelation. The film was filtered and thoroughly washed with double distilled water to make it free from chloride (Cl−) ions. Finally, Ca-alginate film was dried in open air and stored carefully in plastic zipper for further use. In preparation of CQD tailored Ca-alginate film, the as prepared Ca-alginate film was immersed in the aqueous solution of CQDs (30mL, Quantum yield: 16.4%) for 3h to reach the equilibrium state. After that the films were taken out of the solution and dried in open air and restored in zipper for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Vancomycin and β-CD Associated Vancomycin Loaded Carbon Quantum Dot Tailored Calcium Alginate (CA-CQD/VA & CA-CQD/β-VA) Nanohybrid Films
ce:section.ce:para: The glycopeptides antibiotic drug vancomycin was chosen as model drug system in order to showing the applicability of CA-CQD nanohybrid film as drug delivery vehicle. The as prepared CA and CA-CQD films were kept immersed in 30mL of 5mg/mL aqueous solution of vancomycin for 8h to attain the equilibrium state. After that films were taken out and made dry in open air to prepare vancomycin loaded CA-CQD nanohybrid film. To prepare β-CD associated vancomycin (β-VA) complex as model drug systems, equi-molar solutions of β-CD (5.6mg/mL) and vancomycin (7.24mg/mL) were mixed and stirred at 25°C for 48h to attain the homogeneity and equilibrium state. The solution was then filtered using 0.45μm membrane to remove insoluble ingredients (if any). The resulting solution was then frozen at −20°C for 24h. After that it was lyophilized over a period of 36h using a freeze dryer to get the final 1:1 inclusion complex of β-CD with vancomycin (β-VA). The β-VA loaded CA-CQD nanohybrid film was then prepared by immersing the CA and CA-CQD nanohybrid film within 30mL aqueous solution of β-VA (5mg/L) for 8h to reach the equilibrium state. Then films were taken out and made dry in open air.

High resolution transmission electron micrographs (HRTEM) of the prepared CQDs were captured through high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, Tec-nai 12 Philips) operating at 120kV. For specimen preparation, one drop of the CQD aqueous dispersion was dropped on a carbon coated copper grid and dried in vacuum. The hydrodynamic diameter and surface polarity (ξ potential) of the as-synthesized CQDs were evaluated using a Malvern Zeitasizer Nano ZS90. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrophotometer was used to analyze the surface functionalities of CQDs. The FTIR spectra of the specimen CQD was taken through PerkinElmer FT-IR spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA) within the frequency range of 4000–400cm−1 for 42 consecutive scans. For FTIR analysis, sample specimens were prepared by adding several drops of CQD solution on to the solid KBr and transformed it into a pallet form using 10ton hydraulic pressure for 10min. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of the drop casted nanoparticles solution was measured using an Omicron (Series 0571) X-ray photoelectron spectrophotometer. Nano-structural investigation of CQDs was performed using a Rigaku X-ray diffractometer (Model DD966) operating at 40kV/150mA and CuKα (λ=1.54Å) radiation was used as an X-ray source. The Raman spectrum of the aqueous solution of CQDs was recorded at room temperature through a Renishaw InVia Raman microscope, United Kingdom. The UV–vis absorption spectra were characterized using Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrophotometer. All fluorescence spectra were obtained on fluoroMax-4 spectrofluorometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon). Most widely used Time Correlated Single-photon counting (TCSPC) method was employed to determine the fluorescence lifetimes of synthesized CQDs using a HORIBA Jobin Yvon Fluorocube-O1-NL fluorescence lifetime spectrophotometer, where, the sample was excited using a laser diode at 375nm. Output signals were collected at the magic angle of 54.7° to eliminate any considerable contribution from fluorescence anisotropy decay. The timing parameter of our experiment was set up to 100ps and decay traces were deconvoluted using DAS-6 decay analysis software. The acceptability of the fits was judged by correlation co-efficient factor (χ2) and from the visual inspection of the residuals of the fitted junction of the data. Cell imaging was examined by confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM, Zesis, 710 3-Channels, Germany). Cytotoxicity evaluation was tested using an enzyme linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) reader (Bio-Rad). The surface morphology of CQD tailored CA film was investigated through scanning electron microscope (SEM) from JEOL-JSM-5800 model. The photoluminescence (PL) study of the solid CA-CQD film was performed through Hitachi F-2500 spectrofluorometer. The thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) of Ca-alginate and CQDs coated Ca-alginate films were performed under nitrogen purge at a heating rate at 10°C/min by TGA apparatus (model DTG-60, Shimadzu Corporation, Japan). To evaluate the final pH of the drug release mediums of Ca-alginate and CQD-Ca-alginate film, pH meter (ELICO L1 120) was used.

Quantum yield evaluation of highly luminescent CQDs was performed according to earlier report by comparing their integrated PL intensities.1 Quinine sulfate in 0.1M H2SO4 (quantum yield, ɸst =54%) was chosen as a standard and represent with subscript “st”. In order to avoid or to minimize inner filter effects, absorbance in 1cm quartz cuvette was kept below 0.10 at an excitation wavelength of 360nm. The calculation of quantum yield was achieved by the following Eq. (1):
(1)
Φ x
=
Φ st
×
I x
/
I st
×
η x 2
/
η st 2
×
A st
/
A x
where, ɸx and ɸst are the quantum yields of unknown CQD and standard quinine sulfate solution. Ix and Ist are the measured integrated PL intensities and ηx and ηst are the refractive index of the respective solvents (ηx =1.33 and ηst =1.33). Ax and Ast are the optical density of CQD and quinine sulfate solution.

The cytotoxicity of CQDs was evaluated by an MTT assay. HeLa cells were seeded and incubated in a 96-well plate at a density of 1×104 cells per well in 180μL of growth medium and incubated for 24h prior to the addition of CQDs solution. Then 20μL filtered with final concentrations of 100, 250, 300, 350 and 500μg/mL were seeded to each well. Untreated cells in growth media were used as the blank control. Cells were incubated for 48h, followed by the addition of 20μL of 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) solution (5mg/mL) in PBS buffer. After further incubation for 4h, the media were removed and replaced with 150μL DMSO to dissolve the violet MTT formazan crystals (shaking for 15min). The absorbance was recorded at 570nm by an enzyme linked immunosorbent assay ELISA micro-plate reader (Bio-Rad). The cell viability (%) was calculated according to the following equation
(2)
Cell viability
%
=
OD 570
sample
/
OD 570
control
× 100
where, OD570 (sample) represent measurements from the wells treated with CQDS and OD 570(control) from the wells treated with DMEM only. All data are represented as the mean of six measurements (±SD)

HeLa cells were seeded in Dulbecco's modified egle medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum, 1% penicillin and streptomycin. The cells were incubated at 37°C in 5% CO2. Before imaging, cells were placed on 35mm tissue culture plates and then incubated in 2.5mL fresh media at 37°C in 5% CO2. The cells were incubated with 200μL CQD solution in the DMEM for 3h. Then the medium was removed and the cells were washed with phosphate buffered saline (PBS, pH=7.4) for 3 times to remove excess CQD. Finally 1mL of isotonic PBS was added to the plate and fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde for 10min. Then labeled cells were examined under a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In-vitro Drug Release Measurement Through CA and CA-CQD Nanohybrid Films
ce:section.ce:para:
The in-vitro drug release behavior of Ca-alginate (CA) (Koo et al., 2011) and CA/CQD nanohybrid films were studied through UV–visible spectrophotometer, taking amphoteric antibiotic vancomycin (VA) and β-CD associated vancomycin (β-VA) as model drug systems. The drug loading capacities of these films were calculated from the Eq. (3):
(3)
Drug loading
%
=
W ald
−
W bld
W bld
where, Wald and Wbld are the weight of the films after and before the loading of antibiotic drugs, vancomycin respectively. After that, these dried films were dipped into 20mL of double distilled water to investigate their release profiles. The pH responsive behaviors of these films were investigated in different pH environments (pH1.5, 3.0, 5.2, 7.2, 8.0 and 10.0). For UV–visible spectroscopic measurements, 3mL aliquots were taken out from the release medium periodically and added back to the original release medium after each spectroscopic measurement to maintain the constant volume of the release medium. The % cumulative drug release of vancomycin (VA) from CA and CA-CQD nanohybrid films were estimated by measuring UV–visible absorption at a wavelength of ~281nm for each sampling (Li et al., 2010b, 2010c, 2010a). The drug loading process is illustrated as Fig. 2 .

Nano-structural investigation of synthesized CQDs is carried out using high resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) to decipher the geometry and particle size distribution. From HRTEM images [Fig. 3 (a), (b) and (c)], the synthesized CQDs are appeared as spherically shaped particles with varying diameter ranging from 1.5–3.7nm. The average hydrodynamic diameter of CQD is 2.67nm, as calculated from the particle size histogram [inset of Fig. 3(a)]. Fig. 3(d) and (e) show the lattice fringe image of CQD with uniform spacing of 0.18nm, corresponds to the (100) diffraction facets of graphitic carbon. The measured zeta potential (ξ) of these CQDs is −19.3mV [Fig. S1], suggesting the negative surface polarity of the CQDs. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) are employed to explore the surface functionalities, structure and elemental composition of these synthesized CQDs. In Fig. 4 (e), the FTIR peak at around 3422cm−1 is assigned for the OH/NH stretching vibrations of CQDs, whereas; the FTIR peaks at 2907cm−1 and 2841cm−1 correspond to the presence of CH stretching mode (Chen et al., 2004). The FTIR peak at 1724cm−1 confirms the presence of >CO group on the CQD surface, whereas; the bands at 1636cm−1 is due to the CC stretching mode of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (Fan et al., 2014). The presence of sp2 hybridized CH and CO bond in CQD is confirmed through the FTIR peak at 1381cm−1 and 1096cm−1 respectively. The presence of hydrophilic functional groups such as COOH and NH2 may offer its applicability for biomedical applications.

The XPS spectrum of CQDs [Fig. 4(a)] shows three strong peaks at 285.63, 400.2 and 532.72eV, which are attributed to C (1s), N (1s) and O (1s) respectively. These results indicate that the synthesized CQD contains mainly carbon (41.67%), nitrogen (7.04%) and oxygen (51.21%). Specifically, the deconvolution of C (1s) spectrum [Fig. 4(b)] shows four distinct XPS peaks at 284.7, 286.0, 286.8 and 288.9eV, confirming the presence of CC, CN, CO and CO respectively (Qin et al., 2013a, 2013b). On deconvolution of O (1s) spectrum, [Fig. 4(c)] two peaks at 531.4eV and 533.2eV are observed, indicating the presence of CO and COH/COC groups respectively. The magnified version of N (1s) spectrum shows a peak at 400.2eV due to the presence of NH groups [Fig. 4(d)]. The semicrystalline nature of the synthesized CQDs is reflected through its typical XRD pattern [Fig. S2]. It shows a diffraction peak at 2θ value of 22.8°, corresponding to the (002) crystal planes of these CQDs. The interlayer spacing (d) of these CQDs is calculated by using Bragg's equation, nλ=2dSinθ, where n =1, λ=1.54Å and θ is the Bragg's angle. Calculation shows that, the d spacing of these CQDs is coming around 0.42nm, higher as compared to the graphitic interlayer spacing (0.389nm). It may due to the introduction of oxygenous groups within the graphitic layer of CQDs. This fact is in good agreement with the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of the carbon dots, as shown in Fig. 3(f).

Raman spectrum of these CQDs is performed to assign the presence of aromatic carbons. As shown in Fig. 4(f), the Raman spectrum of CQDs exhibits two major bands at 1383 and 1541cm−1, and can be assigned to the presence of D-band (sp3) and G-band (sp2), respectively. The D band generally, represents a vibration mode of sp3 hybridized orbital of a carbon atom and the G band is ascribed for the vibration mode of a sp2 carbon atom. The % of sp3 and sp2 hybridized carbon atom is guided by the relative area intensity (ID/IG) of the D and G bands and is calculated as 1.2. This value suggests that prepared CQDs exhibit two types of carbon species with comparable amounts which is well correlated with the reported literature8 during one-step synthesis of sulfur doped carbon dots with pH sensitive photoluminescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical Properties of Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of Aloe vera leaf derived CQDs are investigated using UV–visible absorption and photoluminescence (PL) studies. The aqueous dispersion of CQDs in day light appears as transparent, while the application of UV lamp (360nm) in dark shows these CQDs are highly luminescent and emits strong blue fluorescence [inset of Fig. 5(a)]. The generation of the fluorescence properties of the CQDs can be ascribed from the presence of sp2 carbon clusters and surface defects of carbon matrix. The presence of conjugated double bonds within the sp2 cluster strongly influence the band gap and optical property of the CQDs in the UV–visible region. The higher % of sp3 carbon atoms (confirmed through Raman spectra) and presence of oxygen and nitrogen containing groups (confirmed through FTIR spectra) enhance the fluorescence property of the as-synthesized CQDs. The transparent look of CQDs aqueous solution suggests the excellent water dispersibility of as-synthesized CQDs and it may ascribed due to the presence of a large number of hydrophilic functional groups on to the surface of CQDs. Fig. 5(a) shows the typical absorption and fluorescence spectra of the CQDs. The absorption spectrum shows an edge 330nm and a peak at 282nm, which is ascribed to the n–π* transition of the CO band and the π–π* transition of the conjugated CC band (Li et al., 2010b, 2010c, 2010a) PL study shows that the fluorescence emission spectrum of the as-prepared CQDs shows a strong emission peak at 425nm when excited at 360nm [Fig. 5(a)]. Variation in the excitation wavelength from 280nm to 430nm causes the shifting of the emission peak gradually towards higher wavelength (red shift) with the decrement of peak intensity [Fig. 5(b)]. The excitation dependent emission is an intrinsic property of carbon nanoparticles. The fluorescence quantum yield measured against an aqueous solution of quinine sulfate in 0.1M H2SO4 is about 16.4% [Table S1]. The upconverted photoluminescence of the synthesized CQDs is clearly reflected from the Fig. 5(d) and can be considered as a consequence of the multiphoton active process (Russo et al., 2007). In a multiphoton active process, the luminescent CQDs are being capable of absorbing two or more photons simultaneously and leads to the emission of light shorter than the excitation wavelength (anti-Stokes emission) (Cao et al., 2007). The upconvertion behavior of these synthesized CQDs is started to visualize with the excitation of 650nm light and goes on increasing with increasing the excitation wavelength up to 750nm, where it shows the maximum photoluminescence intensity. Furthermore, the increase in the excitation wavelength towards the near infrared (NIR) region (up to 900nm), results a decrease in PL intensity as well as a large red shifting of the emission peak upto 510nm. Sometimes, upconverted photoluminescence of CQDs is occurred from the lowest single state irrespective of the mode of excitation (Jia et al., 2012) and shows almost no shifting in the emission peak. But in the present context, a large shifting in the emission peak is observed and implies that the emission occurs from different excited states of CQDs depending on the excitation wavelength (Zhuo et al., 2012). According to these results CQDs are expected to open new opportunities for cell imaging with two-photon luminescence microscopy as well as high-efficiency catalyst design for applications in bioscience and energy technology (Qin et al., 2013a, 2013b).

Fluorescence lifetime is a characteristic parameter, revealing the time-period of the excited state of synthesized CQDs. Typical nano-order value of fluorescence lifetime is an intrinsic property of these CQDs and entirely depends on the nature of fluorescence site and environment. Fluorescence lifetime is assessed by time resolved photoluminescence measurements. The decay trace [Fig. 5(c)] is fitted using a tri exponential function, Y (t)=A+B1exp (t/τ1)+B2exp (t/τ2)+B3exp (t/τ3) on the basis of nonlinear least square analysis. B1, B2 and B3 are the fractional contributions of time-resolved decay lifetime of τ1, τ2 and τ3. The tri-exponential behavior of the lifetime suggests that three different emissive sites are present. This implies the fluorescence state is due to the graphite center (or conjugated structures) and surface traps. The calculated value of average fluorescence lifetime of these CQDs is in the magnitude of nanoseconds, i.e. 2.93ns and thereby suggests that the as-prepared CQDs can be used for optoelectronic and biological applications. This value is in accordance with the reported values during preparation of CQDs from gelatin (Liang et al., 2013).

The photoluminescence property of the as-prepared CQDs in different pH solutions is measured and shown in Fig. 6 (a, b and c). It is observed that the emission wavelength and intensity of synthesized CQDs are sensitive to the pH values. The observed phenomenon is quite different from the other reported literature (Chen and Yan, 2011), where a slight shift in the emission peak is observed with variation in the pH values. On varying the pH value of CQD solution from 1.74 to 7.20, the fluorescence intensity is linearly increased, whereas; from pH7.20 to 12.0, emission intensity is decreased with slight red shifting [Fig. 6(a)]. The pH values of CQD solution are adjusted by adding NaOH solution. The color of CQD solution is seen to be changed upon the variation of pH values. From acidic pH (1.74) to basic pH (12.0) the color of the CQD solution changes to transparent to yellowish-brown [Fig. 6(b)]. The change in color in pH variation may be assigned from the changes of electronic transitions of π–π* and n–π* in graphite nanodomains by refilling or depleting their valence bands (Qin et al., 2013a, 2013b). The above results show that the CQDs have potential as colorimetric and fluorescent sensors for pH measurement.

The excellent photostability of the as-synthesized CQDs can be depicted from the no change of PL intensity after the continuous irradiation of the CQD solution with UV light for 2h [Fig. S3]. The photoluminescence property of the synthesized CQD has also been investigated in different hydrophilic (ethanol, water, acetone, DMF) and hydrophobic (n-hexane, DCM, chloroform) solvents [Fig. S4]. The observed PL intensity is found to be higher for hydrophilic solvents as compared to hydrophobic solvents. This fact can be attributed from the higher degree of solubility of the synthesized CQDs in hydrophilic solvents due to the presence of OH, NH2 functionalities on the surface of CQDs. In case of n-hexane extracted CQD, as expected no PL emission is observed, whereas; for DCM and chloroform, due the H bonding participation between solvent and CQDs, solubility of CQDs is increased and shows detectable fluorescence emission. This solvent dependency of CQDs is in consistence with the earlier report (Wang et al., 2012).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In-vitro Cytotoxicity Assessment and Optical Imaging of Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) in HeLa Cells
ce:section.ce:para: The potentiality of the synthesized CQDs for optical labeling of in vitro cells or simply cell imaging in HeLa cells has been investigated from the fluorescence microscopic images of cultured cells in presence of CQDs. The cultured HeLa cells show a strong blue emission after 6h of the incubation period with luminescence CQDs [Fig. 7 (b)]. Such strong emission indicates that the treated CQDs are internalized into the cultured cells. Fig. 7(c) shows the merged image of the bright field channels [Fig. 7(a)] and fluorescence image [Fig. 7(b)]. On careful observation from Fig. 5(c), luminescent spots are appeared to be present everywhere, starting from the cell membrane to cytoplasmic area of the HeLa cells.

The cellular uptake of these CQDs with negative surface polarity is occurred in a specific way as the precursor Aloe vera leaf gels are also participating in medication without being disturbed the metabolite system of the host person. Therefore, during internalization of these CQDs in to the HeLa cells, genetic disruption is avoided (Bhunia et al., 2014). The non-specific uptake is generally observed for charged fluorescent nanoparticles with larger particle size (Fowley et al., 2012; Li et al., 2011b, 2011a), whereas; for functionalized nanoparticles, cellular uptake is occurred through receptor mediated endocytosis (Bhunia et al., 2013).

The biocompatibility of the synthesized CQDs is evaluated through MTT assay. From Fig. 7(d), it is observed that, the cell viability (%) of these CQDs are >80% up to 350μg/mL of CQDs of the treated HeLa cells and slightly decreased for 500μg/mL (79%). Not only in HeLa cells, cytotoxicities of the Aloe vera leaf derived CQDs in HepG2 and A-549 cell lines are also assessed through MTT assay [Fig. S5]. Results show that these CQDs are almost no cytotoxic upto the higher doses (350μg/mL and 500μg/mL) of these CQDs to the mentioned cell lines. The most noticeable thing is that at such a high concentration of semiconductor nanocrystals, it cannot be used as cell imaging as high dose of these nanoparticles are highly cytotoxic in nature. Due to the almost non-cytotoxic nature of these CQDs, these can be efficiently used as cell tracking, protein analysis by fluorescence energy transfer (FRET), isolation of biomolecules, bio-imaging and gene technology.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Micro-structural, Optical and Swelling Behaviors of Carbon Dot Tailored Ca-alginate (CA-CQD) Film
ce:section.ce:para: The SEM micrograph of the CA-CQD nanohybrid film [Fig. 8 (d)] shows the high surface roughness with porous morphology, although the small sized CQDs are not clearly visible from the micrographs. The uniform distribution of CQDs within the hydrogel film network is clearly observed from the HRTEM image [Fig. 8(e)]. Fig. 8(a) shows the image of CA-CQD nanohybrid film in day light, whereas; Fig. 8(b) shows the image of CA-CQD film under UV lamp. Due to the co-ordination of functional CQDs with the surface functionality of CA film, the as expected photoluminescence (PL) intensity of bare CQDs is not observed [Fig. 8(b)]. A huge decrease in PL intensity for CA-CQD nanohybrid film is observed. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern [inset of Fig.8(e)] of CA-CQD nanohybrid film also shows the graphitic planes of as-synthesized CQDs. The highly porous structure of CA-CQD nanohybrid film is also related to their enhanced swelling ratio as compared to the virgin Ca-alginate film [Fig. 8(c)]. The swelling ratio of CA/CQD nanohybrid film is observed to be more in neutral phase as compared to the acidic environments [Fig. 8(f)]. It is because of the exchange of proton (H+) with Ca2+ ions in hydrogel network of CA film and thereby, leading to the formation of insoluble alginic acid regions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal Behavior of Calcium Alginate Film and CQD Coated Alginate Film
ce:section.ce:para: TG analysis of Ca-alginate (CA) film and Ca-alginate/carbon quantum dot (CA-CQD) nanohybrid thin films are studied in order to decipher the role of Aloe vera leaf derived carbon dots in thermal stability of the Ca-alginate/carbon dot nanohybrid thin films [Fig. 9 ].

Thermal degradation of Ca-alginate film shows three steps decomposition pattern. The first thermal degradation starting at around 50°C and ranging up to 100°C may be ascribed as the expulsion of water molecules from the Ca-alginate surface and corresponds to maximum weight loss of 20%. In the next step, at 210°C, the weight loss of thin film is attributed to structural degradation and partial oxidation of alginate, whereas; the final degradation is occurring at 425°C and corresponds to complete the oxidation of biogenic compounds within alginate films. The TGA thermogram of Ca2+ cross linked alginate film shows good thermal stability due to the highly cross-linked “egg box” structure. Actually thermal stability of polymers greatly depends on the chain mobility which in turn is affected by intra or intermolecular forces, cross-linking density etc. The 13C NMR and molecular modeling suggests that the charge and size of multivalent ions in “egg box” structure of alginate film greatly affects the intra/intermolecular forces and cross-linking density (Konwar et al., 2015). In case of CA-CQD nanohybrid thin film, thermal degradation also shows the three steps decomposition pattern. But the residue left after complete degradation up to 500°C is 51%, whereas; CA film shows a residual mass of 37%. The improved thermal stability of CA-CQD nanohybrid thin film as compared to CA film may be attributed to the uniform distribution of CQDs within the hydrogel network of Ca-alginate film.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Vancomycin (VA) Drug Loading and pH Responsive Delivery Profiles of CA and CA-CQD Nanohybrid Film
ce:section.ce:para: The pH responsive drug delivery profiles of Ca2+ cross linked alginate (CA) films and Aloe vera leaf gel derived CQD coated Ca-alginate nanohybrid (CA-CQD) films are investigated using UV–visible spectrophotometer in terms of cumulative release (%) of the loaded drugs. In present case, anti-bacterial vancomycin (VA) is taken as model drug system. Before studying the pH responsive behavior of both the films, i.e. CA and CA-CQD nanohybrid films, these are soaked in 20mL aqueous solution of vancomycin (5mg/mL). The % of drug loading capacity of CA and CA-CQD nanohybrid films are measured by the weight difference of the films in loaded and unloaded condition of the drugs. To obtain the drug loading capacities, same test for each sample film is run for three times to verify the reproducibility of the results. It is observed that the drug loading capacity of CA and CA-CQD nanohybrid films are 38% (SD=0.124) and 89% (SD=0.060) respectively. The higher drug loading efficiency of the CQD coated CA film may be ascribed by synergistic effect of CQDs and CA film. In other words, this fact is raised due to stronger H bonding between surface attached OH, NH2 and COOH groups of as-synthesized CQDs in combination with the porous network of CA film. Similar type of observation has been reported earlier (Gogoi and Chowdhury, 2014). Thereby, the positive influence of Aloe vera leaf gel derived CQDs on the drug uptake capacity of CA film is undoubtedly realized. Due to the distribution of CQDs within the of CA hydrogel film, a high enhancement in the uptake property of the CA-CQD nanohybrid film is observed. Fig. 10 shows the pH responsive drug delivery of CA film [Fig. 10(a)] and CA-CQD nanohybrid film [Fig. 10 (b)] in different pH environments such as pH1.5, 3.0, 5.2, 7.2, 8.0 and 10.0. The % cumulative release of VA through CA and CA-CQD drug vehicle is higher at acidic pH, i.e. pH1.5. The comparative analysis of the drug release profiles of both the films in pH1.5 shows that in case of Ca-alginate, only 40.2% of the loaded vancomycin is released in 120h, whereas; for CA-CQD nanohybrid film 72% of the loaded vancomycin is released in the same period of time. The rate of cumulative release of VA is higher for the first 1h of the analysis for both, CA and CA-CQD nanohybrid films. For CA film its value is only 19.4%, whereas; for CA-CQD film, it is 50.4%. The cumulative release percentages of VA through CA-CQD films are always found to be higher as compared to CA films at all other pH environments also (pH3.0, 5.2, 7.2, 8.0 and 10.0). This indicates that the CQD coating onto the surface of CA film increases the release of VA through CA-CQD nanohybrid film.

In basic pH (pH10.0), the % cumulative release of VA is again close to the release rate of the acidic environment (pH3.0). For CA film, the % of the cumulative release of VA is 18% for first 1h and 28% for 120h, whereas; for CA-CQD nanohybrid films, these values are 34.2% and 60.5% respectively. At pH3.0, 5.2, 7.2 and 8.0 the percentages of the cumulative release of VA through CA-CQD films are 65.4%, 59%, 55.4% and 55% respectively. The pH responsive release of VA through CA and CA-CQD nanohybrid films can be accounted from the structural investigations of the loaded CA and CA-CQD nanohybrid films in different pH environments, starting from acidic pH1.5 to basic pH10.0. The pH sensitive behavior of Ca-alginate films at pH1.5 can be assigned from the protonation of ionized COO− groups that are in ionic interactions with the Ca2+ ions in “egg box” model of Ca-alginate, which leads to the shrinkage of CA film. On the other hand, in basic environment, there is a continuous exchange of Ca2+ ions by Na+ ions which has led to disruption of gelling structure of Ca-alginate. Therefore, at two extreme pH environments; CA films show the fastest release rate of antibiotic drug vancomycin (VA). In accounting to the feature of the loaded drug VA, the solubility of VA is higher at lower pH1.5 and basic pH10.0. But in basic environment the active sites (responsible for antibiotic activity) of the drug molecules are disrupted. Hence the drug release profiles of VA loaded CA and CA-CQD films are very crucial. In mild acidic (pH5.2) and basic (pH8.0) release mediums, a strong repulsion between the carboxylate ion (COO−) groups are taking the charge over the hydrogen bonding, formed between COOH and COO− groups of Ca-alginate film. This fact can be attributed from the lesser number of protonation of the nearby carboxylate groups in mild acidic or basic medium. In addition to the above fact, an osmotic pressure is also generated within the gelling network due to the presence of mobile ions. These above two facts can be assumed to be responsible for the loosening of gelling network and thereby slowing down the release rates of the loaded drugs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro Release of VA Through (1:1) Host-Guest Complex of β-CD and VA From CA and CA-CQD Nanohybrid Films at pH1.5
ce:section.ce:para: The well known hosting capacity of “Tub” shaped β-CD to accommodate less water soluble drugs within its lypophilic cavity is taken as the advantages to maintain higher loading capacity as well slow release of the loaded drugs (Li and Loh, 2008). In this investigation, an equi-molar mixture of β-CD and vancomycin is used as model drug system. The β-CD associated drug loading capacities are found to be more as compared to absence of β-CD and these values are 45.2% (SD=0.040) and 96% (SD=0.060) for CA and CA-CQD nanohybrid films respectively. The % cumulative release of the inclusion complex of β-CD and vancomycin (β-VA) is found to be lower for CA-CQD films as compared to the % cumulative release of vancomycin (VA) from CA-CQD nanohybrid films [Fig. 11(d)]. Therefore the effect of β-CD to retard the release rate of VA from CA-CQD nanohybrid films is clearly observed. In connection with β-CD, the release rate of vancomycin from CA film is lowered very much [Fig. 11(c)], only 18.2% of the loaded VA is released in 120h. Although the effect of β-CD on the retardation of VA release rate is also prominent for CA-CQD nanohybrid films, but the total % of cumulative release of VA is higher as compared to CA films [Fig. 11(c) and (d)], i.e. 56%. Fig. 11(a), (b), (c) and (d) shows all the possible comparative drug release profiles of CA and CA-CQD nanohybrid films in presence and absence of β-CD at pH1.5. From overall comparison, it is observed that in association with β-CD, the loading capacity of VA within the CA-CQD film is increased highly whereas; the release rate of vancomycin is lowered.

Due to poor absorption of vancomycin in the gastrointestinal (GI) tract, it is generally administered to the patient intravenously with a standard infusion time of at least 1h to avoid the infusion related adverse effects, instead of oral administration. For a patient with normal creatinine clearance, the volume of distribution is 0.4L/kg (Rybak, 2006). The overall activity of vancomycin is dependent on the several factors such as distribution of the drug molecules to tissue, protein binding and inoculums size. The penetration of vancomycin to cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) is very low (0–3.45mg/L). Vancomycin shows slow anti-bacterial activity with poor penetration to CSF and low MIC (minimum inhibitory concentration) and therefore, frequent infusion for a week is required to achieve the desired activity. These difficulties can be over ruled with oral administration of vancomycin. Recently, colon specific controlled delivery systems for vancomycin are designed with freeze died chitosan salts (Cerchiara et al., 2003), pectin microspheres (Bigucci et al., 2009) and chitosan/pectin polyelectrolyte systems (Bigucci et al., 2008). The pluronic-α-cyclodextrin supramolecular gels are designed for sustained delivery of vancomycin (Simões et al., 2012). The higher drug loading efficiency and slower release rate is a crucial factor for long term drug administration in targeted cells (Li et al., 2011b, 2011a). In our present investigation, there is an aim to deliver the vancomycin orally to overcome the problem of poor absorption to gastrointestinal (GI) tract and to treat pseudomembranous colitis type of disease in which case vancomycin must be given orally to reach the site of infection (Bigucci et al., 2008).

Therefore, Aloe vera leaf derived non-toxic CQD coated CA nanohybrid film can be served as the best choice of long term drug administration in gastrointestinal medium, where the pH value is very close to 1.5. The higher loading efficiency CA-CQD films in absence of β-CD can be attributed to the H bonding between surface attached OH, NH2 and COOH groups of synthesized CQDs and OH, NH2, Cl and NH groups of vancomycin molecules [Fig. S6]. In presence of β-CD molecules, vancomycin molecule is trapped within the cavity of the host β-CD and form a 1:1 host-guest inclusion complex. The outer hydrophilic functionality (CH2OH) of the inclusion complex participates strongly in the H bonding with the surface functionality of CQDs and thereby in association with β-CD loading efficiency of β-VA is increased remarkably. For the similar phenomenon, the release rate of β-VA from the swollen network of CA-CQD film is lowered.

Herein, we describe a simple, green and cost effective route to prepare highly luminescent CQDs with the use of Aloe vera leaf gel as the C-source via carbonization pathway. The as-synthesized CQDs possess excitation, pH and polarity dependent luminescence and upconversion fluorescence behavior. Apart from this unique photoluminescence behavior of CQDs show an excellent aqueous dispersibility and biocompatibility (measured through MTT assay). Hence the synthesized CQDs can serve as a promising material for a new class of bio-sensors, fluorescence probes and biomedical imaging devices. The biocompatible CQDs are effectively coated onto the surface of Ca-alginate film by means of simple solution casting technique. Interestingly, the CQD coating onto the CA film makes it highly thermally stable as compared to pure CA films. The drug loading and release efficiencies of the CA-CQD nanohybrid films are measured and observed to be higher at pH1.5 as compared to pure CA film. To maintain the long term drug administration, drug molecules are made to form 1:1 host-guest inclusion complex (β-VA) and then loaded within the CA and CA-CQD films. In presence of β-CD, the loading efficiency of the CA-CQD film is observed to increase of about 7% with respect to the loading of pure VA. Due to stronger H bonding between surface attached OH, NH2 and COOH groups of as-synthesized CQDs and CH2OH groups of β-VA molecules; the rate of drug release is also lowered and therefore, CA-CQD film network in association with β-CD can be served as the promising drug delivery vehicles for long term drug administration at the lower pH1.5, close to the pH lower region of the stomach.

Authors express their thanks to the University Grant Commission (UGC), New Delhi, India for awarding the Rajiv Gandhi National Fellowship to Niladri Sarkar to pursue doctoral degree. Authors also express their thanks to the Council of Scientific and Industrial Research (CSIR), Government of India for financial support [grand no: 01 (2836)/15/EMR-II]. Dr. Jyotirmayee Dash of Department of Organic Chemistry, Indian Association for the Cultivation of Science (IACS), Kolkata, India is acknowledged for helping during analysis of MTT assay. Authors express their thanks to the Department of Instrumentation and Electronics Engineering, Jadavpur University, Salt Lake Campus, Kolkata 700 098, India for experimental support where Dr. Rashmita Das was working as a PhD Scholar.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.ejps.2017.08.015.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Calcium alginate-carboxymethyl cellulose beads for colon-targeted drug delivery

Nanofibrillar cellulose hydrogel promotes three-dimensional liver cell culture

Controlled release of vancomycin from freeze-dried chitosan salts coated with different fatty acids by spray-drying
Ionic strength and pH reversible response of visible and near-infrared fluorescence of graphene oxide nanosheets for monitoring the extracellular pH
A novel pH-sensitive hydrogel composed of N, O-carboxymethyl chitosan and alginate cross-linked by genipin for protein drug delivery
A green and facile approach for the synthesis of water soluble fluorescent carbon dots from banana juice
Cyclodextrins and their uses: a review
Carbon-based dots co-doped with nitrogen and sulfur for high quantum yield and excitation-independent emission
Photoluminescent carbon dots directly derived from polyethylene glycol and their application for cellular imaging
Highly luminescent biocompatible carbon quantum dots by encapsulation with an amphiphilic polymer

Novel carbon dot coated alginate beads with superior stability, swelling and pH responsive drug delivery
Making graphene luminescent by oxygen plasma treatment
Using biofunctional magnetic nanoparticles to capture vancomycin-resistant enterococci and other gram-positive bacteria at ultralow concentration
Photoluminescence effects of graphitic core size and surface functional groups in carbon dots: COO induced red-shift emission
Evaluation of antioxidant potential of Aloe vera (Aloe barbadensis Miller) extracts
One pot green synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon nanoparticles as fluorescent probes for mercury ions
Waste frying oil as a precursor for one-step synthesis of sulfur-doped carbon dots with pH-sensitive photoluminescence
Interpenetrating hydrogels of O-carboxymethyl tamarind gum and alginate for monitoring delivery of acyclovir

Presence of photoluminescent carbon dots in Nescafe® original instant coffee: applications to bioimaging
Water soluble silicon quantum dots with wavelength-tunable photoluminescence
Discrete thermally responsive hydrogel coated gold nanoparticles for use as drug delivery vehicles
Property relationship of alginate and alginate–carbon dot nanocomposites with bivalent and trivalent cross-linker ions
Green chitosan–carbon dots nanocomposite hydrogel film with superior properties
Robust, functionalizable, nanometer-thick poly (acrylic acid) films spontaneously assembled on oxidized aluminum substrates: structures and chemical properties
Maintaining dimensions and mechanical properties of ionically crosslinked alginate hydrogel scaffolds in vitro
Facile synthesis of highly emissive carbon dots from pyrolysis of glycerol; gram scale production of carbon dots/mSiO2 for cell imaging and drug release
Regional delivery of vancomycin using pluronic F-127 to inhibit methicillin resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) growth in chronic otitis media in vitro and in vivo
Sustained release of vancomycin from polyurethane scaffolds inhibits infection of bone wounds in a rat femoral segmental defect model
Water soluble fluorescent carbon quantum dots and photocatalyst design
Cyclodextrin-based supramolecular architectures: syntheses, structures, and applications for drug and gene delivery
Preparation of carbon quantum dots with tunable photoluminescence by rapid laser passivation in ordinary organic solvents
Engineering surface states of carbon dots to achieve controllable luminescence for solid-luminescent composites and sensitive Be2+ detection
An electrochemical avenue to green-luminescent graphene quantum dots as potential electron-acceptors for photovoltaics
Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots with oxygen-rich functional groups
Easy synthesis of highly fluorescent carbon quantum dots from gelatin and their luminescent properties and applications
Nano-carrier for gene delivery and bioimaging based on carbon dots with PEI-passivation enhanced fluorescence
